definition: shy, uncomfortable or guilty feelings; a feeling of being embarrassed

The original sense was ‘ingenuity, cunning’ (surviving in Scots asingine), hence ‘the product of ingenuity, a plot or snare’, also ‘tool, weapon’, later specifically denoting a large mechanical weapon; which led to the sense ‘a machine’ (mid 17th cent.), used commonly later in combinations such assteam engine,internal-combustion engine.

definition: in, at or to another place or other places

definition: a company or business
origin: late Middle English: from Old French, ‘something undertaken’, feminine past participle (used as a noun) ofentreprendre, based on Latinprendere,prehendere‘to take’.

definition: to arrange for yourself or for somebody else to officially join a course, school, etc.

definition: a large number of cases of a particular disease or medical condition happening at the same time in a particular community
origin: early 17th cent. (as an adjective): from Frenchépidémique, fromépidémie, via late Latin from Greekepidēmia‘prevalence of disease’, fromepidēmios‘prevalent’, fromepi‘upon’ +dēmos‘the people’.

definition: a person who hates somebody or who acts or speaks against somebody/something

definition: that you enjoy watching, listening to, doing or experiencing

origin: mid 17th cent.: from Latinerupt-‘broken out’, from the verberumpere, frome-(variant ofex-) ‘out’ +rumpere‘burst out, break’.

definition: a thing, amount, word, etc. that is equal in value, meaning or purpose to something else

(denoting a person believing that they are divinely inspired): from Frenchenthousiasteor ecclesiastical Latinenthusiastes‘member of a heretical sect’, from Greekenthousiastēs‘person inspired by a god’, from the adjectiveenthous‘possessed by a god, inspired’ (based ontheos‘god’).

definition: one part of a story that is broadcast on television or radio in several parts

definition: to experience and deal with something that is painful or unpleasant without giving up

origin: Old Englishende(noun),endian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutcheinde(noun),einden(verb) and GermanEnde(noun),enden(verb).

definition: connected with the natural conditions in which people, animals and plants live; connected with the environment

definition: to get away from a place where you have been kept as a prisoner or not allowed to leave

definition: to build something

definition: causing great interest or excitement

definition: extremely good

definition: the state of feeling or showing happiness and enthusiasm

definition: a substance that is able or likely to cause an explosion
origin: N/A

definition: to travel to or around an area or a country in order to learn about it
origin: mid 16th cent. (in the sense ‘investigate (why)’): from Frenchexplorer, from Latinexplorare‘search out’, fromex-‘out’ +plorare‘utter a cry’.

definition: every person; all people
origin: N/A

definition: a scientific test that is done in order to study what happens and to gain new knowledge
origin: Middle English: from Old French, or from Latinexperimentum, fromexperiri‘try’. Compare withexperienceandexpert.

definition: covering a large area; great in amount
origin: late Middle English: from Frenchextensif,-iveor late Latinextensivus, fromextens-‘stretched out’, from the verbextendere, fromex-‘out’ +tendere‘stretch’.

definition: to be no longer legally acceptable because the period of time for which it could be used has ended
origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchexpirer, from Latinexspirare‘breathe out’, fromex-‘out’ +spirare‘breathe’.

definition: to show something in a public place for people to enjoy or to give them information
origin: late Middle English (in the sense ‘submit for consideration’, also ‘present a document as evidence in court’): from Latinexhibit-‘held out’, from the verbexhibere, fromex-‘out’ +habere‘hold’.

definition: to kill somebody, especially as a legal punishment
origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchexecuter, from medieval Latinexecutare, from Latinexsequi‘follow up, punish’, fromex-‘out’ +sequi‘follow’.

definition: the selling and transporting of goods to another country
origin: N/A

definition: expert knowledge or skill in a particular subject, activity or job
origin: mid 19th cent.: from French, fromexpert, from Latinexpertus, past participle ofexperiri‘try’.

definition: a situation in which somebody treats somebody else in an unfair way, especially in order to make money from their work
origin: N/A

definition: the state of being in a place or situation where there is no protection from something harmful or unpleasant
origin: early 17th cent.: fromexpose, on the pattern of words such asenclosure.

definition: to consider or study an idea, a subject, etc. very carefully
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchexaminer, from Latinexaminare‘weigh, test’, fromexamen‘examination’.

definition: a way out of a public building or vehicle
origin: mid 16th cent. (as a stage direction): from Latinexit‘he or she goes out’, third person singular present tense ofexire, fromex-‘out’ +ire‘go’. The noun (late 16th cent.) is from Latinexitus‘going out’, from the verbexire, and the other verb uses (early 17th cent.) derive from it.

definition: the act of increasing the area of activity, group of people, etc. that is affected by something
origin: late Middle English: from late Latinextensio(n-), fromextendere‘stretch out’, fromex-‘out’ +tendere‘stretch’.

definition: a person whose opinions, especially about religion or politics, are extreme, and who may do things that are violent or illegal for what they believe
origin: N/A

definition: unexpected, surprising or strange
origin: late Middle English: from Latinextraordinarius, fromextra ordinem‘outside the normal course of events’.

definition: extremely good
origin: late Middle English (in the sense ‘known through fable’): from Frenchfabuleuxor Latinfabulosus‘celebrated in fable’, fromfabula‘story’, fromfari‘speak’.

definition: the money that you spend on something
origin: late Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French, alteration of Old Frenchespense, from late Latinexpensa (pecunia)‘(money) spent’, from Latinexpendere‘pay out’, fromex-‘out’ +pendere‘weigh, pay’.

definition: a feeling, situation, way of behaving, etc. that is as different as possible from another or is opposite to it
origin: late Middle English: via Old French from Latinextremus‘outermost, utmost’, superlative ofexterus‘outer’.

definition: a short passage from a book, piece of music, etc. that gives you an idea of what the whole thing is like
origin: late Middle English: from Latinextract-‘drawn out’, from the verbextrahere, fromex-‘out’ +trahere‘draw’.

definition: material made byweavingwool, cotton, silk, etc., used for making clothes, curtains, etc. and for covering furniture
origin: late 15th cent.: from Frenchfabrique, from Latinfabrica‘something skilfully produced’, fromfaber‘worker in metal, stone, etc.’ The word originally denoted a building, later a machine, the general sense being ‘something made’, hence sense (1) (mid 18th cent., originally denoting any manufactured material). Sense (2) dates from the mid 17th cent.

definition: a reason, either true or invented, that you give to explain or defend your behaviour
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchescuser(verb), from Latinexcusare‘to free from blame’, fromex-‘out’ +causa‘accusation, cause’.

definition: the sudden violentburstingand loud noise of something such as a bomb exploding; the act of deliberately causing something to explode
origin: early 17th cent.: from Latinexplosio(n-)‘scornful rejection’, from the verbexplodere‘drive out by clapping, hiss off the stage’, fromex-‘out’ +plaudere‘to clap’.

definition: in a way that covers a large area
origin: N/A

definition: to be opposite somebody/something; to have your face or front pointing towards somebody/something or in a particular direction
origin: Middle English: from Old French, based on Latinfacies‘form, appearance, face’.

definition: clear and easy to understand, so that you have no doubt what is meant
origin: early 17th cent. (as an adjective): from Frenchexpliciteor Latinexplicitus, past participle ofexplicare‘unfold’, fromex-‘out’ +plicare‘to fold’.

definition: to show or make known a feeling, an opinion, etc. by words, looks or actions
origin: verblate Middle English (also in the sense ‘press out, obtain by squeezing’, used figuratively to mean ‘extort’): from Old Frenchexpresser, based on Latinex-‘out’ +pressare‘to press’.

definition: to tell somebody about something in a way that makes it easy to understand
origin: late Middle English: from Latinexplanare, based onplanus‘plain’.

definition: a word or phrase
origin: late Middle English: from Latinexpressio(n-), fromexprimere‘press out, express’. Compare withexpress(verb).

definition: an organized journey with a particular purpose, especially to find out about a place that is not well known
origin: late Middle English: via Old French from Latinexpeditio(n-), fromexpedire‘extricate’, fromex-‘out’ +pes,ped-‘foot’. Early senses included ‘prompt supply of something’ and ‘setting out with aggressive intent’. The notions of ‘speed’ and ‘purpose’ are retained in current senses. Sense (1) dates from the late 16th cent.

definition: to treat a person or situation as an opportunity to gain an advantage for yourself
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchesploit(noun), based on Latinexplicare‘unfold’, fromex-‘out’ +plicare‘to fold’. The early notion of ‘success, progress’ gave rise to the sense ‘attempt to capture’, ‘military expedition’, hence the current sense of the noun. Verb senses (mid 19th cent.) are from modern Frenchexploiter.

definition: toburst(= break apart)or make somethingburstloudly and violently, causing damage
origin: mid 16th cent. (in the sense ‘reject scornfully’): from Latinexplodere‘drive out by clapping, hiss off the stage’, fromex-‘out’ +plaudere‘to clap’. Sense (7) is derived from the original sense of the word. Sense (1) (late 18th cent.) evolved via an old sense ‘expel with violence and sudden noise’, perhaps influenced by obsoletedisplode‘burst with a noise’.

definition: to think or believe that something will happen or that somebody will do something
origin: mid 16th cent. (in the sense ‘defer action, wait’): from Latinexspectare‘look out for’, fromex-‘out’ +spectare‘to look’ (frequentative ofspecere‘see’).

definition: connected with the family or a particular family
origin: late Middle English (in sense (3); also denoting the servants of a household or the retinue of a nobleman): from Latinfamilia‘household servants, family’, fromfamulus‘servant’.

definition: how large, important, serious, etc. something is
origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘valuation of property, especially for taxation’): from Anglo-Norman Frenchextente, from medieval Latinextenta, feminine past participle of Latinextendere‘stretch out’, fromex-‘out’ +tendere‘stretch’.

definition: moral principles that control or influence a person’s behaviour
origin: late Middle English (denoting ethics or moral philosophy; also used attributively): from Old Frenchéthique, from Latinethice, from Greek(hē) ēthikē (tekhnē)‘(the science of) morals’, based onēthos‘nature, disposition’, (plural) ‘customs’.

definition: to sell and send goods to another country
origin: late 15th cent. (in the sense ‘take away’): from Latinexportare, fromex-‘out’ +portare‘carry’. Current senses date from the 17th cent.

definition: to some extent but not very
origin: N/A

definition: more than is usual, expected, or than exists already
origin: mid 17th cent. (as an adjective): probably a shortening ofextraordinary, suggested by similar forms in French and German.

definition: acceptable and appropriate in a particular situation
origin: adjectiveOld Englishfæger‘pleasing, attractive’, of Germanic origin; related to Old High Germanfagar.

definition: lack of success in doing or achieving something
origin: mid 17th cent. (originally asfailer, in the senses ‘non-occurrence’ and ‘stopping of supply’): from Anglo-Norman Frenchfailerfor Old Frenchfaillir, based on Latinfallere‘deceive’.

definition: to make an action or a process possible or easier
origin: early 17th cent.: from Frenchfaciliter, from Italianfacilitare, fromfacile‘easy’, from Latinfacilis‘easy’, fromfacere‘do, make’.

definition: having knowledge or skill in a particular job or activity
origin: N/A

definition: well known to you; often seen or heard and therefore easy to recognize
origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘intimate’, ‘on a family footing’): from Old Frenchfamilier, from Latinfamiliaris, fromfamilia‘household servants, family’, fromfamulus‘servant’.

definition: the act of spending or using money; an amount of money spent
origin: mid 18th cent.: fromexpend, suggested by obsoleteexpenditor‘officer in charge of expenditure’, from medieval Latin, fromexpenditus, irregular past participle of Latinexpendere, fromex-‘out’ +pendere‘weigh, pay’.

definition: buildings, services, equipment, etc. that are provided for a particular purpose
origin: early 16th cent. (denoting the means or unimpeded opportunity for doing something): from Frenchfacilitéor Latinfacilitas, fromfacilis‘easy’, fromfacere‘do, make’.

definition: clearly or directly, so that the meaning is easy to understand
origin: N/A

definition: trust in somebody’s ability or knowledge; trust that somebody/something will do what has been promised
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchfeid, from Latinfides.

definition: to deliberately not include something in what you are doing or considering
origin: late Middle English: from Latinexcludere, fromex-‘out’ +claudere‘to shut’.

definition: a pleasant situation that you imagine but that is unlikely to happen
origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchfantasie, from Latinphantasia, from Greek ‘imagination, appearance’, later ‘phantom’, fromphantazein‘make visible’. From the 16th to the 19th cents the Latinized spellingphantasywas also used.

definition: to show something that is usually hidden
origin: N/A

definition: done with, having or involving great knowledge or skill
origin: Middle English (as an adjective): from French, from Latinexpertus, past participle ofexperiri‘try’. The noun use dates from the early 19th cent. Compare withexperienceandexperiment.

definition: the business of managing or working on a farm
origin: N/A

definition: easily able or likely to explode
origin: N/A

definition: a person who has an important job as a manager of a company or an organization
origin: late Middle English: from medieval Latinexecutivus, fromexsequi, fromex-‘out’ +sequi‘follow’.

definition: very great in degree
origin: late Middle English: via Old French from Latinextremus‘outermost, utmost’, superlative ofexterus‘outer’.

definition: to use land for growing crops and/or keeping animals
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchferme, from medieval Latinfirma‘fixed payment’, from Latinfirmare‘fix, settle’ (in medieval Latin ‘contract for’), fromfirmus‘constant, firm’; compare withfirm(noun). The noun originally denoted a fixed annual amount payable as rent or tax; which later gave rise to ‘to subcontract’ (farm somebody/​something out to somebody.). The verb sense ‘grow crops or keep livestock’ dates from the early 19th cent.

definition: physical or mental activity that you do to stay healthy or become stronger
origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘application of a right’): via Old French from Latinexercitium, fromexercere‘keep busy, practise’, fromex-‘thoroughly’ +arcere‘keep in or away’.

definition: one of several things that cause or influence something
origin: late Middle English (meaning ‘doer’, also in the Scots sense ‘agent’): from Frenchfacteuror Latinfactor, fromfact-‘done’, from the verbfacere.

definition: a building or group of buildings where goods are made, mainly by machine
origin: late 16th cent. (originally referring to a place where traders did business in a foreign country): via Portuguesefeitoria. The current sense is based on late Latinfactorium, literally ‘oil press’.

definition: the money that you pay to travel by bus, plane, taxi, etc.
origin: Old Englishfær,faru‘travelling, a journey or expedition’,faran‘to travel’, also ‘get on (well or badly’), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvarenand Germanfahren‘to travel’, Old Norseferja‘ferry boat’, also toford. Senses 1 and 2 of the noun stem from an earlier meaning ‘a journey for which a price is paid’. Noun sense 3 was originally used with reference to the quality or quantity of food provided, probably from the idea of faring well or badly.

definition: at a greater distance away from you
origin: Old Englishfeorr, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchver, from an Indo-European root shared by Sanskritparaand Greekpera‘further’.

definition: a popular style of clothes, hair, etc. at a particular time or place; the state of being popular
origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘make, shape, appearance’, also ‘a particular make or style’): from Old Frenchfaçon, from Latinfactio(n-), fromfacere‘do, make’.

definition: to become or to make something become paler or less bright
origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘grow weak’): from Old Frenchfader, fromfade‘dull, insipid’, probably based on a blend of Latinfatuus‘silly, insipid’ andvapidus‘vapid’.

definition: the state of being sent to live in another country that is not your own, especially for political reasons or as a punishment
origin: Middle English: the noun partly from Old Frenchexil‘banishment’ and partly from Old Frenchexile‘banished person’; the verb from Old Frenchexiler; all based on Latinexilium‘banishment’, fromexul‘banished person’.

definition: to want something or want to do something
origin: late Middle English: contraction offantasy.

definition: known about by many people
origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchfameus, from Latinfamosus‘famed’, fromfama.

definition: not what somebody claims it is; appearing to be something it is not
origin: late 18th cent. (originally slang): origin uncertain; perhaps ultimately related to Germanfegen‘sweep, thrash’.

definition: moving or able to move quickly
origin: adjectiveOld Englishfæst‘firmly fixed, steadfast’ andfæste‘firmly’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvastand Germanfest‘firm, solid’ andfast‘almost’. In Middle English the adverb developed the senses ‘strongly, vigorously’ (compare withrun hard), and ‘close, immediate’ (just surviving in the archaicfast by; compare withhard by), hence ‘closely, immediately’ and ‘quickly’; the idea of rapid movement was then reflected in adjectival use.

definition: extremely interesting and attractive
origin: N/A

definition: the state of being known and talked about by many people
origin: Middle English (also in the sense ‘reputation’, which survives inhouse of ill fame): via Old French from Latinfama.

definition: unusually complicated, often in an unnecessary way; intended to impress other people
origin: late Middle English: contraction offantasy.

definition: the act of travelling through a place in order to find out about it or look for something in it
origin: mid 16th cent. (denoting an investigation): from French, or from Latinexploratio(n-), from the verbexplorare‘search out’, fromex-‘out’ +plorare‘utter a cry’. The current sense dates from the early 19th cent.

definition: a department or group of related departments in a college or university
origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchfaculte, from Latinfacultas, fromfacilis‘easy’, fromfacere‘make, do’.

definition: to a very high degree
origin: N/A

definition: following a style that is popular at a particular time
origin: N/A

definition: an area of land, and the buildings on it, used for growing crops and/or keeping animals
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchferme, from medieval Latinfirma‘fixed payment’, from Latinfirmare‘fix, settle’ (in medieval Latin ‘contract for’), fromfirmus‘constant, firm’; compare withfirm(noun). The noun originally denoted a fixed annual amount payable as rent or tax; which later gave rise to ‘to subcontract’ (farm somebody/​something out to somebody.). The noun came to denote ‘a lease’, and, in the early 16th cent., ‘land leased for farming’.

definition: wrong; not correct or true
origin: Old Englishfals‘fraud, deceit’, from Latinfalsum‘fraud’, neuter past participle offallere‘deceive’; reinforced or re-formed in Middle English from Old Frenchfals,faus‘false’.

definition: having too muchfleshon it and weighing too much
origin: Old Englishfǣtt‘well fed, plump’, also ‘fatty, oily’, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchvetand Germanfeist.

definition: connected with or located on the outside of something/somebody
origin: late Middle English: from medieval Latin, from Latinexter‘outer’.

definition: the front part of the head, where the eyes, nose and mouth are
origin: Middle English: from Old French, based on Latinfacies‘form, appearance, face’.

definition: a person or thing that you like more than the others of the same type
origin: late 16th cent. (as a noun): from obsolete Frenchfavorit, from Italianfavorito, past participle offavorire‘to favour’, from Latinfavor, fromfavere‘show kindness to’ (related tofovere‘cherish’).

definition: to close or join together the two parts of something; to become closed or joined together
origin: Old Englishfæstnian‘make sure’, also ‘immobilize’, of West Germanic origin; related tofast‘quick’.

definition: a thing that you do to help somebody
origin: Middle English (in the noun sense ‘liking, preference’): via Old French from Latinfavor, fromfavere‘show kindness to’ (related tofovere‘cherish’).

definition: to forgive somebody for something that they have done, for example not being polite or making a small mistake
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchescuser(verb), from Latinexcusare‘to free from blame’, fromex-‘out’ +causa‘accusation, cause’.

definition: a thing that is added to something that is not usual, standard or necessary and that costs more
origin: mid 17th cent. (as an adjective): probably a shortening ofextraordinary, suggested by similar forms in French and German.

definition: a collection of things, for example works of art, that are shown to the public
origin: late Middle English (in the sense ‘maintenance, support’; hence sense (4), mid 17th cent.): via Old French from late Latinexhibitio(n-), from Latinexhibere‘hold out’, fromex-‘out’ +habere‘hold’.

definition: a person who admires somebody/something or enjoys watching or listening to somebody/something very much
origin: nounsenses 2 to 3 Old Englishfann(as a noun denoting a device for winnowing grain),fannian(verb), from Latinvannus‘winnowing fan’. Compare withvane.nounsense 1 late 19th cent. (originally US): abbreviation offanatic.

definition: extremely good; excellent
origin: late Middle English (in the sense ‘unreal’): from Old Frenchfantastique, via medieval Latin from Greekphantastikos, fromphantazein‘make visible’,phantazesthai‘have visions, imagine’, fromphantos‘visible’ (related tophainein‘to show’). From the 16th to the 19th cents the Latinized spellingphantasticwas also used.

definition: to remove or obtain a substance from something, for example by using an industrial or a chemical process
origin: late Middle English: from Latinextract-‘drawn out’, from the verbextrahere, fromex-‘out’ +trahere‘draw’.

definition: a small group of people within a larger one, whose members have some different aims and beliefs to those of the larger group
origin: senses 1 to 2 late 15th cent. (denoting the action of doing or making something): via French from Latinfactio(n-), fromfacere‘do, make’.sense 3 1960s: blend offactandfiction.

definition: not successful
origin: N/A

definition: a white or yellow substance in the bodies of animals and humans, stored under the skin
origin: Old Englishfǣtt‘well fed, plump’, also ‘fatty, oily’, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchvetand Germanfeist.

definition: to prefer one system, plan, way of doing something, etc. to another
origin: Middle English (in the noun sense ‘liking, preference’): via Old French from Latinfavor, fromfavere‘show kindness to’ (related tofovere‘cherish’).

definition: quickly
origin: adverbOld Englishfæst‘firmly fixed, steadfast’ andfæste‘firmly’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvastand Germanfest‘firm, solid’ andfast‘almost’. In Middle English the adverb developed the senses ‘strongly, vigorously’ (compare withrun hard), and ‘close, immediate’ (just surviving in the archaicfast by; compare withhard by), hence ‘closely, immediately’ and ‘quickly’; the idea of rapid movement was then reflected in adjectival use.

definition: having or based on the belief that women should have the same rights and opportunities as men
origin: late 19th cent.: from Frenchféministe, from Latinfemina‘woman’.

definition: the quality of treating people equally or in a way that is reasonable
origin: N/A

definition: an act of falling
origin: Old Englishfallan,feallan, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvallenand Germanfallen; the noun is partly from the verb, partly from Old Norsefall‘downfall, sin’.

definition: to not be successful in achieving something
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchfaillir(verb),faille(noun), based on Latinfallere‘deceive’.

definition: having a system of government in which the individual states of a country have control over their own affairs, but are controlled by a central government for national decisions, etc.
origin: mid 17th cent.: from Latinfoedus,foeder-‘league, covenant’ +-al.

definition: to include a particular person or thing as a special feature
origin: late Middle English (originally denoting the form or proportions of the body, or a physical feature): from Old Frenchfaiture‘form’, from Latinfactura‘formation, manufacture’, fromfacere‘do, make’.

definition: the 2nd month of the year, between January and March
origin: Middle Englishfeverer, from Old Frenchfeverier, based on Latinfebruarius, fromfebrua, the name of a purification feast held in this month. The spelling change in the 15th cent. was due to association with the Latin word.

definition: a structure made of wood or wire supported with posts that is put between two areas of land as aboundary, or around a garden, field, etc. to keep animals in, or to keep people and animals out
origin: Middle English (in the sense ‘defending, defence’): shortening ofdefence. Compare withfend.

definition: a group consisting of one or two parents and their children
origin: late Middle English (in sense (3); also denoting the servants of a household or the retinue of a nobleman): from Latinfamilia‘household servants, family’, fromfamulus‘servant’.

definition: to drop down from a higher level to a lower level
origin: Old Englishfallan,feallan, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvallenand Germanfallen; the noun is partly from the verb, partly from Old Norsefall‘downfall, sin’.

definition: the bad feeling that you have when you are in danger or when a particular thing frightens you
origin: Old Englishfǣr‘calamity, danger’,fǣran‘frighten’, also ‘revere’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchgevaarand GermanGefahr‘danger’.

definition: being a woman or a girl
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchfemelle, from Latinfemella, diminutive offemina‘a woman’. The change in the ending was due to association withmale, but the wordsmaleandfemaleare not linked etymologically.

definition: a type of literature that describes imaginary people and events, not real ones
origin: late Middle English (in the sense ‘invented statement’): via Old French from Latinfictio(n-), fromfingere‘form, contrive’. Compare withfeignandfigment.

definition: causing or ending in death
origin: late Middle English (in the senses ‘destined by fate’ and ‘ominous’): from Old French, or from Latinfatalis, fromfatum‘that which has been spoken’, fromfari‘speak’.

definition: used to describe somebody who is the same as you in some way, or in the same situation
origin: N/A

definition: an animal that can lay eggs or give birth to babies; a plant that can produce fruit
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchfemelle, from Latinfemella, diminutive offemina‘a woman’. The change in the ending was due to association withmale, but the wordsmaleandfemaleare not linked etymologically.

definition: liked more than others of the same kind
origin: late 16th cent. (as a noun): from obsolete Frenchfavorit, from Italianfavorito, past participle offavorire‘to favour’, from Latinfavor, fromfavere‘show kindness to’ (related tofovere‘cherish’).

definition: a thing that is known to be true, especially when it can be proved
origin: late 15th cent.: from Latinfactum, neuter past participle offacere‘do’. The original sense was ‘an act’, later ‘a crime’, surviving in the phrasebefore (or after) the fact. The earliest of the current senses (‘truth, reality’) dates from the late 16th cent.

definition: to give food to a person or an animal
origin: Old Englishfēdan(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvoedenandfood.

definition: an amount of money that you pay for professional advice or services
origin: Middle English: from an Anglo-Norman French variant of Old Frenchfeu,fief, from medieval Latinfeodum,feudum, ultimately of Germanic origin. Compare withfeudandfief.

definition: a small number of people, things or places; some
origin: Old Englishfēawe,fēawa, of Germanic origin; related to Old High Germanfao, from an Indo-European root shared by Latinpaucusand Greekpauros‘small’.

definition: the act of giving food to a person, an animal or a plant
origin: N/A

definition: a male parent of a child or an animal; a person who is acting as the father to a child
origin: Old Englishfæder, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvaderand GermanVater, from an Indo-European root shared by Latinpaterand Greekpatēr.

definition: to make something longer or larger
origin: late Middle English: from Latinextendere‘stretch out’, fromex-‘out’ +tendere‘stretch’.

definition: a person who supports the belief that women should have the same rights and opportunities as men
origin: late 19th cent.: from Frenchféministe, from Latinfemina‘woman’.

definition: a person who owns or manages a farm
origin: late Middle English: from Old Frenchfermier, from medieval Latinfirmarius,firmator, fromfirma‘fixed payment’, from Latinfirmare‘fix, settle’ (in medieval Latin ‘contract for’), fromfirmus‘constant, firm’. The current sense originally denoted ‘a bailiff’ or ‘steward who farmed land on the owner's behalf’, or ‘a tenant farmer’.

definition: one of the many soft light parts covering a bird’s body
origin: Old Englishfether, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchveerand GermanFeder, from an Indo-European root shared by Sanskritpatra‘wing’, Latinpenna‘feather’, and Greekpteron,pterux‘wing’.

definition: making people have a good opinion of somebody/something
origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latinfavorabilis, fromfavor, fromfavere‘show kindness to’ (related tofovere‘cherish’).

definition: to be frightened of somebody/something or frightened of doing something
origin: Old Englishfǣr‘calamity, danger’,fǣran‘frighten’, also ‘revere’, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchgevaarand GermanGefahr‘danger’.

definition: something important, interesting or typical of a place or thing
origin: late Middle English (originally denoting the form or proportions of the body, or a physical feature): from Old Frenchfaiture‘form’, from Latinfactura‘formation, manufacture’, fromfacere‘do, make’.
N/A

definition: the things, especially bad things, that will happen or have happened to somebody/something
origin: late Middle English: from Italianfatoor (later) from its source, Latinfatum‘that which has been spoken’, fromfari‘speak’.

definition: to make a film of a story or a real event; to record somebody/something on film
origin: Old Englishfilmen‘membrane’, of West Germanic origin.

definition: being or happening at the end of a series of events, actions, statements, etc.
origin: Middle English (in the adjectival sense ‘conclusive’): from Old French, or from Latinfinalis, fromfinis‘end’. Compare withfinish.

definition: a meal of milk for a young baby; a meal for an animal
origin: Old Englishfēdan(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchvoedenandfood.

definition: an action or a piece of work that needs skill, strength or courage
origin: late Middle English (in the general sense ‘action or deed’): from Old Frenchfait, from Latinfactum, neuter past participle offacere‘do’.

definition: after a long time, especially when there has been some difficulty or delay
origin: N/A

definition: in a strong or definite way
origin: N/A

definition: the feeling you get when you touch something or are touched

definition: the man that somebody is married to; a married man

definition: to move or act quickly because there is not much time
origin: late 16th cent. (as a verb): imitative.

definition: of or connected with people rather than animals, machines or gods
origin: late Middle Englishhumaine, from Old Frenchhumain(e), from Latinhumanus, fromhomo‘man, human being’. The present spelling became usual in the 18th cent.; compare withhumane.

definition: the organ in the chest that sends blood around the body, usually on the left in humans
origin: Old Englishheorte, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhartand GermanHerz, from an Indo-European root shared by Latincor,cord-and Greekkēr,kardia.

definition: to fasten or hang something on something else using ahook; to be fastened or hanging in this way
origin: Old Englishhōc, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhoek‘corner, angle, projecting piece of land’, also to GermanHaken‘hook’.

definition: the part of the body on top of the neck containing the eyes, nose, mouth and brain
origin: Old Englishhēafod, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhoofdand GermanHaupt.

definition: an idea or explanation of something that is based on a few known facts but that has not yet been proved to be true or correct

late Old English, fromhund‘hundred’ (from an Indo-European root shared with Latincentumand Greekhekaton) + a second element meaning ‘number’; of Germanic origin and related to Dutchhonderdand Germanhundert.

definition: the country where a person was born
origin: N/A

definition: people in general
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchhumanite, from Latinhumanitas, fromhumanus, fromhomo‘man, human being’.

definition: in what way or manner
origin: Old Englishhū, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchhoe, also towhoandwhat.

definition: believing that something you want will happen
origin: N/A

definition: to do something that shows great respect for somebody/something
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchonor(noun),onorer(verb), from Latinhonos,honor.

definition: a large animal with four legs, amane(= long thick hair on its neck) and a tail.Horsesare used for riding on, pullingcarriages, etc.
origin: Old Englishhors, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchrosand GermanRoss.

definition: the quality of being honest
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchhoneste, from Latinhonestas, fromhonestus, fromhonos,honor. The original sense was ‘honour, respectability’, later ‘decorum, virtue, chastity’.

definition: houses, flats, etc. that people live in, especially when referring to their type, price or condition
origin: N/A

definition: a curved piece of metal, plastic or wire for hanging things on, catching fish with, etc.
origin: Old Englishhōc, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhoek‘corner, angle, projecting piece of land’, also to GermanHaken‘hook’.

definition: all the events that happened in the past
origin: late Middle English (also as a verb): via Latin from Greekhistoria‘finding out, narrative, history’, fromhistōr‘learned, wise man’, from an Indo-European root shared bywit‘have knowledge’.

definition: to have somebody/something in your hand, arms, etc.
origin: verbOld Englishhaldan,healdan, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhoudenand Germanhalten; the noun is partly from Old Norsehald‘hold, support, custody’.

definition: a chemical element.Hydrogenis a gas that is the lightest of all the elements. It combines withoxygento form water.
origin: late 18th cent.: coined in French from Greekhudro-‘water’ +-genēs‘-born, of a specified kind’, fromgen-(root ofgignomai‘be born, become’,genos‘a kind’).

definition: perfect; most suitable
origin: late Middle English (as a term in Platonic philosophy, in the sense ‘existing as an archetype’): from late Latinidealis, via Latin from Greekidea‘form, pattern’, from the base ofidein‘to see’.

definition: a small symbol on a computer or smartphone screen that represents a program or a file
origin: mid 16th cent. (in the sense ‘simile’): via Latin from Greekeikōn‘likeness, image’. Current senses date from the mid 19th cent. onwards.

definition: a person rather than an animal or a machine
origin: late Middle Englishhumaine, from Old Frenchhumain(e), from Latinhumanus, fromhomo‘man, human being’. The present spelling became usual in the 18th cent.; compare withhumane.

definition: the state of not having enough food to eat, especially when this causes illness or death
origin: Old Englishhungor(noun),hyngran(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhongerand GermanHunger.

definition: a building where people stay, usually for a short time, paying for their rooms and meals
origin: mid 18th cent.: from Frenchhôtel, from Old Frenchhostel, from medieval Latinhospitale, neuter of Latinhospitalis‘hospitable’, fromhospes,hospit-‘host, guest’.

definition: to organize an event to which others are invited and make all the arrangements for them
origin: verbMiddle English: from Old Frenchhoste, from Latinhospes,hospit-‘host, guest’.

definition: a period of time when you are not at work or school

definition: an act of looking for somebody/something that is difficult to find

definition: a person who is captured and held prisoner by a person or group, and who may be injured or killed if people do not do what the person or group is asking
origin: Middle English: from Old French, based on late Latinobsidatus‘the state of being a hostage’ (the earliest sense in English), from Latinobses,obsid-‘hostage’.

definition: great respect for somebody
origin: Middle English: from Old Frenchonor(noun),onorer(verb), from Latinhonos,honor.

definition: an idea or standard that seems perfect, and worth trying to achieve or obtain
origin: late Middle English (as a term in Platonic philosophy, in the sense ‘existing as an archetype’): from late Latinidealis, via Latin from Greekidea‘form, pattern’, from the base ofidein‘to see’.

definition: 60 minutes; one of the 24 parts that a day is divided into

definition: a lack of knowledge or information about something
origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latinignorantia, fromignorant-‘not knowing’, from the verbignorare‘not know, ignore’, fromin-‘not’ +gno-, a base meaning ‘know’.

definition: a feeling of unhappiness because somebody has been unkind or unfair to you
origin: Middle English (originally in the senses ‘ to strike’ and ‘a blow’): from Old Frenchhurter(verb),hurt(noun), perhaps ultimately of Germanic origin.

definition: a rude way to refer to somebody who you think is very stupid

definition: not allowed by the law
origin: early 17th cent.: from Frenchillégalor medieval Latinillegalis, from Latinin-‘not’ +legalis‘according to the law’.

definition: the process of showing, proving or recognizing who or what somebody/something is
origin: mid 17th cent.: originally from medieval Latinidentificat-‘identified’, from the verbidentificare; later fromidentify.

definition: to cause physical pain to somebody/yourself; to injure somebody/yourself
origin: Middle English (originally in the senses ‘ to strike’ and ‘a blow’): from Old Frenchhurter(verb),hurt(noun), perhaps ultimately of Germanic origin.

definition: based on or connected with anideology
origin: N/A

definition: to bring your hand, or an object you are holding, against somebody/something quickly and with force
origin: late Old Englishhittan(in the sense ‘come upon, find’), from Old Norsehitta‘come upon, meet with’, of unknown origin.

definition: a building for people to live in, usually for one family
origin: Old Englishhūs(noun),hūsian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhuis, GermanHaus(nouns), and Dutchhuizen, Germanhausen(verbs).

definition: to provide a place for a person or an animal to live
origin: Old Englishhūs(noun),hūsian(verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchhuis, GermanHaus(nouns), and Dutchhuizen, Germanhausen(verbs).

definition: to recognize somebody/something and be able to say who or what they are
origin: mid 17th cent. (in the sense ‘treat as being identical with’): from medieval Latinidentificare, from late Latinidentitas(from Latinidem‘same’) + Latin-ficare(fromfacere‘make’).

definition: a large building where people who are ill or injured are given medical treatment and care
origin: Middle English (originally meaning a hospice run by the Knights Hospitallers in the 11th century): via Old French from medieval Latinhospitale, neuter of Latinhospitalis‘hospitable’, fromhospes,hospit-‘host, guest’.

definition: a set of ideas that an economic or political system is based on
origin: late 18th cent. (originally referring to the science of ideas): from Frenchidéologie, from Greekidea‘form, pattern’ +-logos(denoting discourse or compilation).

definition: existing only in your mind or imagination
origin: late Middle English: from Latinimaginarius, fromimago,imagin-‘image’.

definition: to make something that has been officially decided start to happen or be used
origin: late Middle English (in the sense ‘article of furniture, equipment, or dress’): partly from medieval Latinimplementa(plural), partly from late Latinimplementum‘filling up, fulfilment’, both from Latinimplere‘fill up’ (later ‘employ’), fromin-‘in’ + Latinplere‘fill’. The verb dates from the early 18th cent.

definition: similar in every detail

definition: to suggest that something is true or that you feel or think something, without saying so directly

definition: an official way of showing who you are, for example a document with your name, date of birth and often a photograph on it (the abbreviation for‘identity’or‘identification’)
origin: N/A

definition: a feeling of great shock or fear

definition: aggressive or unfriendly feelings or behaviour

definition: to go after wild animals in order to catch or kill them for food, sport or to make money

definition: a picture, photograph or statue that represents somebody/something

definition: a violent storm with very strong winds, especially in the western Atlantic Ocean

definition: aggressive or unfriendly and ready to argue or fight
origin: late 16th cent.: from French, or from Latinhostilis, fromhostis‘stranger, enemy’.

definition: the state of being physically or mentally ill
origin: N/A

definition: the quality in something that makes it funny; the ability to laugh at things that are funny

The original sense was ‘bodily fluid’ (surviving inaqueous humourandvitreous humour); it was used specifically for any of the cardinal humours (sense (3)), which led to the sense ‘mental disposition’ (thought to be caused by the relative proportions of the humours). This led, in the 16th cent., to the senses ‘mood’ (sense (2)) and ‘whim’, henceto humour someone‘to indulge a person's whim’. Sense (1) dates from the late 16th cent.

definition: suffering from an illness or disease; not feeling well
origin: Middle English (in the senses ‘wicked’, ‘malevolent’, ‘harmful’, and ‘difficult’): from Old Norseillr‘evil, difficult’, of unknown origin.

definition: the need or wish to get something done quickly

definition: a person who has come to live permanently in a different country from the one they were born in
origin: late 18th cent.: from Latinimmigrant-‘immigrating’, from the verbimmigrare, on the pattern ofemigrant.

definition: the fact of not being able to do something
origin: N/A

definition: extremely large or great

definition: an idea, a feeling or an opinion that you get about somebody/something, or that somebody/something gives you
origin: late Middle English: via Old French from Latinimpressio(n-), fromimpress-‘pressed in’, from the verbimprimere, fromin-‘into’ +premere‘to press’.

definition: a possible effect or result of an action or a decision

definition: not enough; not good enough
origin: N/A

definition: language that produces pictures in the minds of people reading or listening

definition: annoyed by somebody/something, especially because you have to wait for a long time

definition: to form a picture in your mind of what something might be like

definition: to pay no attention to something
origin: late 15th cent. (in the sense ‘be ignorant of’): from Frenchignoreror Latinignorare‘not know, ignore’, fromin-‘not’ +gno-, a base meaning ‘know’. Current senses date from the early 19th cent.

definition: water that has frozen and become solid
origin: Old Englishīs, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchijsand GermanEis.

definition: used as the subject of a verb when the speaker or writer is referring to himself/herself

definition: wanting to do something
origin: N/A

definition: the quality of being important
origin: early 16th cent.: from French, from medieval Latinimportantia, fromimportant-‘being of consequence’, from the verbimportare‘bring in’ (in medieval Latin ‘imply, mean, be of consequence’), fromin-‘in’ +portare‘carry’.

definition: funny; showing a sense of humour
origin: N/A

definition: feeling that you want to eat something
origin: Old Englishhungrig, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchhongerig, Germanhungrig, also tohunger.

definition: the ability to create pictures in your mind; the part of your mind that does this
origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latinimaginatio(n-), from the verbimaginari‘picture to oneself’, fromimago,imagin-‘image’.

definition: into an object, an area or a substance

definition: to become better than before; to make something/somebody better than before
origin: early 16th cent. (asemproweorimprowe): from Anglo-Norman Frenchemprower(based on Old Frenchprou‘profit’, ultimately from Latinprodest‘is of advantage’);-owewas changed to-oveunder the influence ofprove. The original sense was ‘make a profit, increase the value of’; subsequently ‘make greater in amount or degree’.

definition: to introduce a new law, rule, tax, etc.; to order that a rule, punishment, etc. be used
origin: late 15th cent. (in the sense ‘impute’): from Frenchimposer, from Latinimponere‘inflict, deceive’ (fromin-‘in, upon’ +ponere‘put’), but influenced byimpositus‘inflicted’ and Old Frenchposer‘to place’.

definition: if one thingincludesanother, it has the second thing as one of its parts

definition: the act of putting somebody in a prison or another place from which they cannot escape; the state of being there

definition: the fact of including somebody/something; the fact of being included

definition: admiring somebody/something because you think they are particularly good, interesting, etc.

definition: the powerful effect that something has on somebody/something

definition: happening not as the main aim, cause or result of a particular action, but in addition to it
origin: late Middle English (in the sense ‘not in full grammatical concord’): from medieval Latinindirectus, fromin-‘not’ +directus(past participle ofdirigere, fromdi-‘distinctly’ orde-‘down’ +regere‘put straight’).

definition: to use pictures, photographs, diagrams, etc. in a book, etc.
origin: early 16th cent. (in the sense ‘illuminate, shed light on’): from Latinillustrat-‘lit up’, from the verbillustrare, fromin-‘upon’ +lustrare‘illuminate’.

definition: a type of sweet frozen food made from milk fat, tasting of fruit, chocolate, etc. and often eaten as adessert; a small amount of this food intended for one person, often served in a container made of biscuit that is like aconein shape

definition: making you admire them, because they are very large, good,skilful, etc.

definition: the money that a person, a region, a country, etc. earns from work, from investing money, from business, etc.

definition: who or what somebody/something is

definition: that cannot exist or be done; not possible

definition: to become greater in amount, number, value, etc.; to make something greater in amount, number, value, etc.
origin: Middle English (formerly also asencrease): from Old Frenchencreistre, from Latinincrescere, fromin-‘into’ +crescere‘grow’.

definition: the act of making something that has been officially decided start to happen or be used
origin: N/A

definition: to put somebody in a prison or another place from which they cannot escape
origin: Middle Englishemprison, from Old Frenchemprisoner, fromem-‘in’ +prison.

definition: as is certain to happen
origin: N/A

definition: having a great effect on people or things; of great value

definition: going after and killing wild animals as a sport or for food
origin: N/A

definition: used afterveryand an adjective or adverb to emphasize a statement, description, etc.
origin: Middle English: originally asin deed.

definition: a plan, thought or suggestion, especially about what to do in a particular situation
origin: late Middle English (as a term in Platonic philosophy): via Latin from Greekidea‘form, pattern’, from the base ofidein‘to see’.

definition: more and more all the time
origin: N/A

definition: a product or service that is brought into one country from another

definition: a drawing or picture in a book, magazine, for decoration or to explain something

definition: to allow yourself to have or do something that you like, especially something that is considered bad for you

definition: to reach an opinion or decide that something is true on the basis of information that is available

definition: the act or process of causing or getting a disease

definition: used to say that one thing can, will or might happen or be true, depending on another thing happening or being true

definition: a rise in the amount, number or value of something

definition: to have an effect on somebody/something

definition: something that encourages you to do something

definition: to bring a product or service into one country from another

definition: to include something so that it forms a part of something

definition: the act of making something better; the process of something becoming better
origin: late Middle Englishemprowement(in the sense ‘profitable management or use; profit’), from Anglo-Norman French, fromemprower(based on Old Frenchprou‘profit’, ultimately from Latinprodest‘is of advantage’).

definition: considered separately rather than as part of a group

definition: to receive money, property, etc. from somebody when they die

definition: if a person or thingimpressesyou, you admire them or it

definition: freedom from political control by other countries

definition: connected with industry

definition: the basic systems and services that are necessary for a country or an organization to run smoothly, for example buildings, transport and water and power supplies

definition: a unit for measuring length or height, equal to 2.54centimetres. There are 12inchesin a foot.

definition: a person or an animal that lives in a particular place

definition: having its own government
origin: early 17th cent. (as an adjective): partly on the pattern of Frenchindépendant.

definition: without delay
origin: N/A

definition: at a point within an area or a space
origin: Old Englishin(preposition),inn,inne(adverb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutch and Germanin(preposition), Germanein(adverb), from an Indo-European root shared by Latininand Greeken.

definition: well known for being bad or evil

definition: a sign that a system, society, etc. is very bad or very wrong

definition: not guilty of a crime, etc.; not having done something wrong

definition: one of the things from which something is made, especially one of the foods that are used together to make a particular dish

definition: contained as part of something
origin: N/A

definition: having something as part of a group or set
origin: N/A

definition: inside or into a building

definition: to make a disease or an illness spread to a person, an animal or a plant
origin: late Middle English: from Latininfect-‘tainted’, from the verbinficere, fromin-‘into’ +facere‘put, do’.

origin: Middle English: of unknown origin.

definition: to want something to happen or to be true even though it is unlikely or impossible
origin: Old Englishwȳscan, of Germanic origin; related to Germanwünschen, also towont.

definition: the day before today
origin: Old Englishgiestran dæg(fromyester-Old Englishgeostran, of Germanic origin +day).

definition: a person whose job is writing books, stories, articles, etc.
origin: Old Englishwrītere, of Germanic origin.

definition: used in negative sentences and questions to talk about something that has not happened but that you expect to happen
origin: Old Englishgīet(a), of unknown origin.

definition: the period from 1 January to 31 December, that is 365 or 366 days, divided into 12 months
origin: Old Englishgē(a)r, of Germanic origin; related to Dutchjaarand GermanJahr, from an Indo-European root shared by Greekhōra‘season’.

definition: the colour of lemons or butter
origin: Old Englishgeolu,geolo, of West Germanic origin; related to Dutchgeeland Germangelb, also togold.

definition: an amount of something that has the value mentioned
origin: Old Englishw(e)orth(adjective and noun), of Germanic origin; related to Dutchwaardand Germanwert.

definition: an area or a region with a particular feature or use
origin: late Middle English: from French, or from Latinzona‘girdle’, from Greekzōnē.

definition: used to answer a question and say that something is correct or true
origin: Old Englishgēse,gīse, probably from an unrecorded phrase meaning ‘may it be so’.
